question: Why is multer.memoryStorage() dangerous for large file uploads?

options.text: The entire file is stored in RAM as a Buffer -- uploading a 500MB video causes OOM (Out of Memory)
Memory storage is slower than disk storage
options.explanation: Actually, memory storage is faster (RAM is faster than disk). The problem isn't speed but memory consumption. For 100 users uploading 100MB each = 10GB RAM

options.text: Memory storage doesn't support multiple files
options.explanation: Memory storage supports single, array, and fields. The problem is each file is fully loaded into RAM. For small files (<5MB), this is fine

options.text: Files are lost on server restart
options.explanation: True, but not the danger. Files in memory storage are always processed immediately (saved to disk/S3). The problem is RAM consumption during upload

question: Why can't you trust file.originalname when saving a file?

options.text: The original name can contain path traversal (../../etc/passwd) and overwrite system files
The filename might be too long for the filesystem
options.explanation: Length is a secondary concern. The main threat is path traversal. The name '../../etc/passwd' allows writing files outside the uploads/ directory

options.text: Non-ASCII characters in the filename can cause encoding errors
options.explanation: Encoding is a minor issue. The main concern is security. Always generate UUIDs for filenames and store the original name in DB metadata

options.text: Two users might upload files with the same name
options.explanation: Name collision is a problem, but not the main one. Path traversal allows an attacker to overwrite any file on the server, including configuration files

question: Why is magic byte checking better than file extension checking?

options.text: Magic bytes are signatures at the start of a file that can't be spoofed by simple renaming (.exe -> .jpg)
Magic bytes check files faster than extension checking
options.explanation: Extension checking is faster (simple string comparison). Magic bytes require reading the first bytes. The advantage is reliability, not speed

options.text: File extensions aren't supported in Linux
options.explanation: Linux supports extensions, though it doesn't use them to determine type. The issue is that extensions are just part of the name that users can change

options.text: Magic bytes only work for images
options.explanation: Magic bytes exist for most formats: PDF (%PDF), ZIP (PK), JPEG (FF D8 FF), PNG (89 50 4E 47). The file-type library recognizes hundreds of formats

question: How does busboy save RAM compared to Multer when uploading files?

options.text: Busboy processes files in a streaming fashion (chunks of ~64KB), not loading the entire file into memory

options.explanation: Busboy doesn't compress files. It parses the multipart stream and passes chunks via Node.js Stream API. RAM savings come from not buffering the entire file

options.text: Busboy uses a swap file instead of RAM
options.explanation: Busboy doesn't use swap. It pipes data from the request stream to the write stream. Only the current chunk (~64KB) is in memory

options.text: Busboy is faster than Multer, so the file passes through RAM in less time
options.explanation: Speed doesn't affect RAM consumption. Multer memoryStorage holds the entire Buffer until processing. Busboy passes chunks as received -- only the current chunk is in RAM
